message:
chore: sync concurrent agent changes

Multi-agent environment sync.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-3i0q","title":"Idea: Project-local .rchignore excludes","description":"## Background
Projects often need custom excludes beyond global config (large datasets, generated artifacts, secrets). A project-local ignore file aligns with familiar workflows and reduces transfer size/risk.

## Goals
- Support `.rchignore` in project root with gitignore-style globs.
- Merge with existing transfer exclude patterns (config + defaults).
- Keep behavior deterministic and visible in verbose logs.

## Design / Approach
- Read `.rchignore` if present; parse line-based patterns (ignore comments/blank lines).
- Merge into transfer_config.exclude_patterns with stable ordering.
- Provide `rch diagnose` / verbose hook logging to show effective excludes count.

## Tasks / Subtasks
- Add parser for `.rchignore` lines.
- Integrate into transfer pipeline and mock rsync layer.
- Update docs (README + config guide) with examples and precedence.

## Tests
- Unit: parse behavior (comments, blank lines, whitespace).
- Integration: `.rchignore` overrides applied to rsync call (mock).
- E2E: excluding a large fixture reduces transfer size.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `.rchignore` works without config changes.
- Effective excludes are visible in verbose mode.
- No regression in default exclude handling.

## Logging & E2E
- E2E script: scripts/e2e_bd-3i0q.sh (one script per bead unless intentionally combined).
- Logging format: JSONL via TestLogger (tests/true_e2e/logging.rs) or scripts/test_lib.sh log_json.
- Required fields: ts, test, phase, worker, command, bytes_transferred, duration_ms, result, error (if any).
- Phases to log (as applicable): classify, select, sync_up, exec, sync_down, cleanup, summary.
- Include a final summary block with pass/fail counts and total elapsed time.

## Edge Cases & UX
- Ignore comments/blank lines; trim whitespace.
- No full gitignore negation support (document that `!` is literal for now).
- Merge order is deterministic: defaults + config + .rchignore.

## E2E Outline
- Add large file + .rchignore pattern -> rsync excludes it.
- Remove .rchignore -> file is transferred again.
- Verbose output shows effective exclude count.

## Unit Tests (Detailed)
- rch/src/transfer.rs: .rchignore parser handles comments/blank lines/whitespace.
- rch/src/transfer.rs: merge order deterministic.

## E2E Script Notes
- scripts/e2e_bd-3i0q.sh: create large file, ignore it, verify rsync excludes.
- Remove .rchignore and verify file transfers again.

 {"id":"bd-3ib0","title":"Unify worker state terminology across CLI","description":"# Unify Worker State Terminology

## Problem
The CLI uses confusing, overlapping terms for worker states:

### Current Commands
- `rch workers drain css` - Stops sending new jobs, waits for current to finish
- `rch workers disable css` - Also stops sending jobs? How different from drain?
- `rch workers enable css` - Resumes sending jobs

### User Confusion
Users don't understand:
1. What's the difference between drain and disable?
2. Is 'paused' the same as 'drained'?
3. Can a disabled worker be drained?
4. What's the opposite of drain - 'undrain' or 'enable'?

## Proposed Terminology

### Worker States (clear hierarchy)
```
ENABLED (normal operation)
  ↓ rch workers drain
DRAINING (accepting no new jobs, finishing current)
  ↓ (all jobs complete)
DRAINED (idle, no jobs, ready to disable)
  ↓ rch workers disable
DISABLED (completely offline, skipped by selection)
```

### Commands
```
rch workers drain <id>     Start graceful drain
rch workers enable <id>    Resume from drained/draining
rch workers disable <id>   Mark worker as offline (must be drained first)
rch workers status <id>    Show detailed worker state
```

### Remove/Deprecate
- Remove 'pause' terminology entirely
- Deprecate direct 'disable' without drain (or require --force)

## Documentation Updates
- Update --help text for all worker commands
- Add state diagram to rch workers --help
- Update README.md worker management section

## Migration
1. Add deprecation warning to confusing usages
2. Keep old behavior for 1-2 releases
3. Remove deprecated paths

## Files to Modify
- rch/src/main.rs (command definitions, help text)
- rch/src/commands.rs (worker state handling)
- rch/src/status_display.rs (status output)
- README.md (documentation)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:06:08.311137466Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:46:46.819516487Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T22:46:46.819450474Z","close_reason":"Added Drained state to WorkerStatus enum with automatic transition from Draining when all jobs complete. Updated theme, UI, API, health, alerts, telemetry, workers, and selection to handle new state. Added tests.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-3jru","title":"Implement MetricsDashboard for periodic stats display","description":"Create MetricsDashboard in rchd/src/ui/metrics.rs for performance summary:
- Periodic statistics panel (configurable interval, default 5min)
- Jobs completed, failed, cache hits in period
- Average job duration with trend indicator (↑↓→)
- Worker utilization percentages
- Transfer volume (bytes synced up/down)

Technical requirements:
- Only display when stderr is TTY and rich output enabled
- Use Table for metrics with right-aligned numbers
- Include sparkline-style trend indicators using Unicode blocks
- Calculate and display percentiles (p50, p95, p99) for latency
- Reset counters option with --metrics-reset-interval
- Support Prometheus-compatible metric names in description

Example periodic output:
╭─ Metrics (last 5 minutes) ───────────────────╮
│ Jobs: 47 completed, 2 failed (96% success)   │
│ Cache: 38 hits, 9 misses (81% hit rate)      │
│ Avg Duration: 23.4s (↓ improving)            │
│ Workers: 89% utilized │ Queue peak: 12 jobs  │
│ Transfer: ↑ 1.2 GB  ↓ 3.4 GB                 │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────────╯","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:07:56.185396572Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T03:46:04.682633418Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T03:46:04.682566413Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3jru","depends_on_id":"bd-1z6p","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-26T20:11:38Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3jru","depends_on_id":"bd-3u68","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-26T20:11:38Z","created_by":"import"}]}
+{"id":"bd-3jsw","title":"WS1.5: (Optional) Add in-memory cache for TimingHistory","description":"FOLLOW-UP OPTIMIZATION after WS1.1-WS1.4 are complete.

PROBLEM: spawn_blocking moves I/O off the async thread but doesn't reduce latency. If the timing history file is slow to read (HDD, cold cache, large file), the decision path can still exceed the 5ms budget.

SOLUTION: Keep TimingHistory in memory with write-through to disk.

Implementation:
1. Add a static or process-global cache: OnceCell<RwLock<TimingHistory>>
2. On first access (lazy load): spawn_blocking(load) and store in cache
3. estimate_timing_for_build: Read from cache (no disk I/O)
4. record_build_timing: Update cache AND spawn_blocking(save) for persistence
5. Process restart: Cache is cold, first access loads from disk

Benefits:
- Decision path has zero disk I/O after first load
- Writes are async and don't block decisions
- Survives process restart (data persisted to disk)

Risks:
- Cache stale if file is edited externally (acceptable for this use case)
- Memory usage (timing history is small, ~KB)

This is P3 because spawn_blocking in WS1.1-WS1.2 is sufficient for most cases. This optimization is for high-throughput scenarios.

File: rch/src/hook.rs (TimingHistory impl, new cache module)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:39.699768337Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:49.032659385Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3jsw","depends_on_id":"bd-3s1j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:49.032620242Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3jsw","depends_on_id":"bd-4iov","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:39.710067243Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3jzt","title":"WS1.3: Verify no other blocking I/O in hook path","description":"Audit rch/src/hook.rs for any remaining std::fs or std::io blocking calls in async-reachable code paths.

Check:
- timing_history_path() — does it do I/O? (likely just path construction, OK)
- Any other file reads/writes in the hook pipeline
- Config file loading at startup (acceptable if only at init, not per-request)

Grep for: std::fs::, std::io::Read, std::io::Write, File::open, File::create

Produce a list of all blocking I/O call sites and classify each as:
- FIXED (by WS1.1/WS1.2)
- OK (only at startup/init)
- NEEDS FIX (on hot path, not yet addressed)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:07.391997045Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T22:50:19.927160999Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3jzt","depends_on_id":"bd-1gz8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:38:23.153797391Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3jzt","depends_on_id":"bd-4iov","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:07.399135162Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3jzt","depends_on_id":"bd-mnhp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:38:24.945039499Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3knb","title":"Add rich_rust dependency to workspace Cargo.toml","description":"# Add rich_rust dependency to workspace Cargo.toml

## Task Description

Add rich_rust as a workspace dependency in the root Cargo.toml, then add it to each relevant crate's Cargo.toml (rch, rchd, rch-common).

## Background

rich_rust is located at /dp/rich_rust during development. It provides:
- Console struct for terminal output
- Style and Color types for styling
- Table, Panel, Tree, ProgressBar renderables
- Markup parsing ([bold red]text[/])
- Automatic terminal capability detection

## Implementation Steps

### 1. Add to workspace Cargo.toml

```toml
[workspace.dependencies]
# During development, use path:
rich_rust = { path = "/dp/rich_rust", features = ["full"] }

# When published, switch to:
# rich_rust = { version = "0.1", features = ["full"] }
```

### 2. Add to rch/Cargo.toml

```toml
[dependencies]
rich_rust = { workspace = true, optional = true }

[features]
default = ["rich-ui"]
rich-ui = ["rich_rust"]
```

### 3. Add to rchd/Cargo.toml

Same as rch/Cargo.toml.

### 4. Add to rch-common/Cargo.toml

```toml
[dependencies]
# Minimal features for shared types
rich_rust = { workspace = true, optional = true, default-features = false }

[features]
default = ["rich-ui"]
rich-ui = ["rich_rust"]
```

## Technical Notes

### Why "full" features?
The full feature set includes:
- syntax: Syntax highlighting (syntect)
- markdown: Markdown rendering (pulldown-cmark)
- json: JSON pretty-printing (serde_json)

We want all of these for maximum capability.

### Why optional?
Making rich_rust optional allows:
1. Compile-time removal if issues arise
2. Smaller binaries for users who dont need rich output
3. Faster compilation without feature

### Path vs Published
During development, use path dependency for rapid iteration.
Switch to crates.io version when rich_rust is published.

## Verification

```bash
# Should compile successfully
cargo check --all-targets

# Should compile without rich-ui
cargo check --all-targets --no-default-features

# Should compile with rich-ui
cargo check --all-targets --features rich-ui
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. [ ] Workspace Cargo.toml has rich_rust in [workspace.dependencies]
2. [ ] rch/Cargo.toml has rich_rust with optional + feature flag
3. [ ] rchd/Cargo.toml has rich_rust with optional + feature flag
4. [ ] rch-common/Cargo.toml has rich_rust with minimal features
5. [ ] cargo check passes with default features
6. [ ] cargo check passes with --no-default-features","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:00:51.267049982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T22:33:17.865319328Z","closed_at":"2026-01-19T22:33:17.865271618Z","close_reason":"Added rich_rust as workspace dependency with optional feature flags in rch, rchd, and rch-common. Fixed unicode-width version conflict and clippy warnings in selection.rs.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3knb","depends_on_id":"bd-38kz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-26T20:11:38Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3mog","title":"WS2.4: Unit tests for zero-allocation reject path","description":"Add tests that verify zero heap allocations on the reject path:

1. Classification correctness tests:
   - Test all existing test cases still pass after Cow conversion
   - Specifically test that known non-compilation commands (ls, cat, echo, git status) produce correct Classification with Cow::Borrowed reason
   - Test that compilation commands (cargo build, make, gcc) produce correct Classification

2. Allocation-free reject path verification:
   - Use a custom allocator counter (or #[global_allocator] with counting) to verify that classify_command("ls") produces zero heap allocations
   - Alternative: use std::hint::black_box + criterion benchmark showing 0 allocs
   - At minimum: verify the reason field is Cow::Borrowed (not Cow::Owned) for reject cases

3. Cow roundtrip tests:
   - Verify Classification.reason can be displayed, compared, serialized/deserialized
   - Verify ClassificationTier.name and .reason work with serde (Cow<str> serializes as string)

Test location: rch-common/src/patterns.rs (inline #[cfg(test)] module)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:52:50.341239385Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T22:53:07.233080728Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3mog","depends_on_id":"bd-25cj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:53:07.233042126Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3mog","depends_on_id":"bd-3uo1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:52:50.348245311Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3mog","depends_on_id":"bd-54u8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:53:05.430228370Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
@@ -258,7 +259,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-3v9b","title":"WS3.1: Replace speed_score RwLock with AtomicU64","description":"Convert speed_score: RwLock<f64> (line 25) to AtomicU64 using f64::to_bits/from_bits.

Replace:
  pub speed_score: RwLock<f64>,
With:
  pub speed_score: AtomicU64,  // stores f64 via to_bits/from_bits

Update accessor (line 160-162):
  pub fn get_speed_score(&self) -> f64 {
      f64::from_bits(self.speed_score.load(Ordering::Relaxed))
  }
  pub fn set_speed_score(&self, v: f64) {
      self.speed_score.store(v.to_bits(), Ordering::Relaxed);
  }

This removes the async requirement from get_speed_score() — it no longer needs .await.
Update all callers in selection.rs to remove .await on speed_score reads.

File: rchd/src/workers.rs (struct def line 25, accessor lines 160-162), rchd/src/selection.rs","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:42.019414801Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T22:51:09.783263916Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3v9b","depends_on_id":"bd-2f5o","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:42.032290446Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-466a","title":"Systemd unit: enable --now + log hints","description":"Switch to systemctl --user enable --now rchd.service when available (fallback to enable + start if needed). Update installer summary to reference systemctl status and journalctl follow. Improves perceived automation and discoverability.","acceptance_criteria":"Installer uses systemctl --user enable --now (with safe fallback) and shows status/log commands; service starts without extra manual steps.","notes":"Maintain idempotence: re-running installer should restart or no-op cleanly.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T22:02:29.815947408Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T22:09:02.680859411Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T22:09:02.680841467Z","close_reason":"Updated enable/start flow and hints","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-4fgv","title":"WS3.2: Replace scalar RwLocks with atomics","description":"Convert hot-path scalar RwLock fields to atomics:

1. last_latency_ms: RwLock<Option<u64>> (line 27) → AtomicU64
   - CONFIRMED HOT PATH: accessed in selection.rs:1405 during worker scoring
   - Use 0 as None sentinel (latency of 0ms is not meaningful)
   - get: let v = self.last_latency_ms.load(Relaxed); if v == 0 { None } else { Some(v) }
   - set: self.last_latency_ms.store(ms.unwrap_or(0), Relaxed);

LOWER PRIORITY (not on selection hot path, but could still benefit):

2. disabled_at: RwLock<Option<i64>> (line 39) → AtomicI64
   - NOT in selection.rs hot path (only used in workers.rs enable/disable)
   - Still worth converting for consistency
   - Use 0 as None sentinel

NOTE: consecutive_failures and total_builds do NOT exist as direct fields on WorkerState. They are tracked inside CircuitStats. Do not try to extract them — the circuit RwLock stays.

Update all read/write sites. Remove .await from accessor calls that become non-async.

Files affected:
- rchd/src/workers.rs (struct def, accessors at lines ~282, 300, 337)
- rchd/src/selection.rs (line 1405 uses last_latency_ms)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:43.816481648Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T23:47:09.741168207Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4fgv","depends_on_id":"bd-2f5o","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:43.822921234Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-4fgv","depends_on_id":"bd-3v9b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:38:28.555005498Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-4iov","title":"WS1: Eliminate Blocking File I/O in Async Hook","description":"PROBLEM: rch/src/hook.rs uses std::fs::read_to_string (line 886) and std::fs::write (line 906) in TimingHistory::load() and ::save(). These are called from synchronous functions record_build_timing() (line 948) and estimate_timing_for_build() (line 983). While the callers themselves are sync, they are invoked from the async hook pipeline, meaning they block the current tokio thread for the duration of disk I/O.

Additionally, save() uses std::fs::create_dir_all (line 901) and std::fs::rename (line 907).

WHY IT MATTERS: The hook is the critical path for every command invocation. When these sync I/O calls execute on a tokio worker thread, they block that thread from processing other async tasks. On slow disks or under I/O pressure, this can stall concurrent operations.

SOLUTION: Wrap the sync I/O calls in tokio::task::spawn_blocking(). This keeps the methods synchronous (minimal cascading changes) while offloading the blocking work to tokio's dedicated blocking thread pool.

FILES: rch/src/hook.rs (TimingHistory impl, lines 873-910, callers at 948, 983)
RISK: Low — spawn_blocking is a mechanical wrapper change.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:36:02.705249793Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T22:54:16.799356438Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4iov","depends_on_id":"bd-2mmt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:36:02.718993284Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":79,"issue_id":"bd-4iov","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"BACKGROUND: The RCH hook runs as a PreToolUse handler in Claude Code. Every tool invocation triggers it. The async runtime (tokio) processes the hook decision. When std::fs calls block the runtime thread, ALL concurrent async operations stall — not just this one hook call. On a busy system with multiple concurrent tool invocations, this can cascade.

MEASUREMENT: std::fs::read_to_string on a cold file can take 50-500μs depending on disk. tokio::fs moves this to a blocking thread pool, keeping the async executor free. The latency doesn't decrease, but the concurrency impact disappears entirely.

VALIDATION: After conversion, run `cargo test` in the rch crate. No behavioral changes expected — only the execution context changes from blocking-inline to blocking-offloaded.","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:38:49Z"},{"id":83,"issue_id":"bd-4iov","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CORRECTION FROM REVIEW: The original bead claimed load/save were async fns. They are actually synchronous methods called from sync callers (record_build_timing, estimate_timing_for_build). However, these sync callers ARE invoked from the async hook pipeline — so the blocking still occurs on a tokio worker thread.

The fix is spawn_blocking, NOT converting to tokio::fs. spawn_blocking is simpler:
- No need to make TimingHistory methods async
- No need to propagate async up the call chain
- tokio's blocking thread pool handles the I/O
- If the blocking pool is full, it spawns new threads (up to 512 by default)

This is the standard tokio pattern for unavoidable sync I/O in async contexts.","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:54:16Z"}]}
+{"id":"bd-4iov","title":"WS1: Eliminate Blocking File I/O in Async Hook","description":"PROBLEM: rch/src/hook.rs uses std::fs::read_to_string (line 886) and std::fs::write (line 906) in TimingHistory::load() and ::save(). These are called from synchronous functions record_build_timing() (line 948) and estimate_timing_for_build() (line 983). While the callers themselves are sync, they are invoked from the async hook pipeline, meaning they block the current tokio thread for the duration of disk I/O.

Additionally, save() uses std::fs::create_dir_all (line 901) and std::fs::rename (line 907).

WHY IT MATTERS: The hook is the critical path for every command invocation. When these sync I/O calls execute on a tokio worker thread, they block that thread from processing other async tasks. On slow disks or under I/O pressure, this can stall concurrent operations.

SOLUTION: Wrap the sync I/O calls in tokio::task::spawn_blocking(). This keeps the methods synchronous (minimal cascading changes) while offloading the blocking work to tokio's dedicated blocking thread pool.

FILES: rch/src/hook.rs (TimingHistory impl, lines 873-910, callers at 948, 983)
RISK: Low — spawn_blocking is a mechanical wrapper change.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:36:02.705249793Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:21.894966195Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4iov","depends_on_id":"bd-2mmt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:36:02.718993284Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":79,"issue_id":"bd-4iov","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"BACKGROUND: The RCH hook runs as a PreToolUse handler in Claude Code. Every tool invocation triggers it. The async runtime (tokio) processes the hook decision. When std::fs calls block the runtime thread, ALL concurrent async operations stall — not just this one hook call. On a busy system with multiple concurrent tool invocations, this can cascade.

MEASUREMENT: std::fs::read_to_string on a cold file can take 50-500μs depending on disk. tokio::fs moves this to a blocking thread pool, keeping the async executor free. The latency doesn't decrease, but the concurrency impact disappears entirely.

VALIDATION: After conversion, run `cargo test` in the rch crate. No behavioral changes expected — only the execution context changes from blocking-inline to blocking-offloaded.","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:38:49Z"},{"id":83,"issue_id":"bd-4iov","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"CORRECTION FROM REVIEW: The original bead claimed load/save were async fns. They are actually synchronous methods called from sync callers (record_build_timing, estimate_timing_for_build). However, these sync callers ARE invoked from the async hook pipeline — so the blocking still occurs on a tokio worker thread.

The fix is spawn_blocking, NOT converting to tokio::fs. spawn_blocking is simpler:
- No need to make TimingHistory methods async
- No need to propagate async up the call chain
- tokio's blocking thread pool handles the I/O
- If the blocking pool is full, it spawns new threads (up to 512 by default)

This is the standard tokio pattern for unavoidable sync I/O in async contexts.","created_at":"2026-01-29T22:54:16Z"},{"id":88,"issue_id":"bd-4iov","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"RISK ASSESSMENT for estimate_timing_for_build (line 1225):

This function calls TimingHistory::load() on the DECISION PATH, which has a <5ms budget. File I/O adds latency:
- Best case (cached, SSD): ~50-200μs
- Typical case (SSD): ~500μs - 2ms  
- Worst case (HDD, cold): 5-50ms (EXCEEDS BUDGET!)

MITIGATION OPTIONS:

1. spawn_blocking (current plan): Moves I/O to blocking thread pool, but the decision still WAITS for the result. If I/O is slow, decision is slow.

2. In-memory cache (recommended enhancement): Load timing history ONCE at daemon startup (or lazily on first access) and keep in memory. Update cache on record_build_timing. Write-through to disk in background.

3. Async preload: At process start, kick off a background task to load timing history. By the time first classification happens, it's likely ready. If not ready, use default (fail-open).

RECOMMENDATION: spawn_blocking is a MINIMUM FIX. Consider adding an in-memory cache as a follow-up optimization. File this as a separate bead if scope creep is a concern.

For now, spawn_blocking protects against blocking the tokio runtime (which affects ALL concurrent operations). Even if the I/O is slow, only THIS hook invocation is slow, not the entire daemon.","created_at":"2026-01-29T23:48:21Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-59kg","title":"Unit Tests: Self-Healing System","description":"# Unit Tests: Self-Healing System

## Overview
Comprehensive unit tests for all self-healing components to ensure correctness and prevent regressions.

## Test Organization

### Test File Structure
```
rch/src/
├── hook.rs                    # Add tests at bottom
├── hook/autostart.rs          # Or separate module
├── doctor.rs                  # Add fix tests
└── config.rs                  # Config parsing tests

rch_common/src/
└── hooks.rs                   # Hook utility tests

rchd/src/
└── hooks.rs                   # Hook installation tests

tests/
└── self_healing_unit.rs       # Integration-style unit tests
```

## Test Categories

### 1. Hook Auto-Start Tests (for bd-qsr3)

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_acquires_lock
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_acquires_lock() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: no lock file, no socket
    assert!(!test_env.lock_file().exists());
    assert!(!test_env.socket_path().exists());
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let config = SelfHealingConfig::default();
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
    
    // Assert: lock file was created during execution
    // (Note: lock is released after, so check logs or use mock)
    assert!(result.is_ok() || matches!(result, Err(AutoStartError::BinaryNotFound)));
}
```

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_respects_existing_lock
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_respects_existing_lock() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: create lock file (simulating concurrent start)
    let _lock = acquire_lock(test_env.lock_file()).unwrap();
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let config = SelfHealingConfig::default();
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
    
    // Assert: returns LockHeld error
    assert!(matches!(result, Err(AutoStartError::LockHeld)));
}
```

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_respects_cooldown
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_respects_cooldown() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: create cooldown file with recent timestamp
    write_cooldown_timestamp(test_env.cooldown_file());
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let config = SelfHealingConfig {
        auto_start_cooldown: 30,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
    
    // Assert: returns CooldownActive error
    assert!(matches!(result, Err(AutoStartError::CooldownActive(_, _))));
}
```

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_cooldown_expired
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_cooldown_expired() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: create cooldown file with old timestamp (>30s ago)
    write_cooldown_timestamp_at(test_env.cooldown_file(), 
        SystemTime::now() - Duration::from_secs(60));
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let config = SelfHealingConfig {
        auto_start_cooldown: 30,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
    
    // Assert: does NOT return CooldownActive (attempts start)
    assert!(!matches!(result, Err(AutoStartError::CooldownActive(_, _))));
}
```

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_detects_stale_socket
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_detects_stale_socket() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: create a fake socket file (not a real socket)
    std::fs::write(test_env.socket_path(), "").unwrap();
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let config = SelfHealingConfig::default();
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
    
    // Assert: stale socket was removed (or StaleSocket error with removal)
    // The function should detect the socket isn't responding and remove it
}
```

#### Test: try_auto_start_daemon_config_disabled
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_auto_start_config_disabled() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: config with hook_starts_daemon = false
    let config = SelfHealingConfig {
        hook_starts_daemon: false,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    
    // Action: call try_auto_start_daemon()
    let result = try_auto_start_daemon(&config, test_env.socket_path()).await;
```

### 2. Daemon Hook Installation Tests (for bd-1emo)

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_already_installed
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_already_installed() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: create valid settings.json with hook already present
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    test_env.write_settings_json(json!({
        "hooks": {
            "PreToolUse": [
                {"command": "rch", "description": "RCH hook"}
            ]
        }
    }));
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: true };
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config).unwrap();
    
    // Assert: returns AlreadyInstalled
    assert!(matches!(result, HookResult::AlreadyInstalled));
    
    // Assert: file unchanged
    let settings = test_env.read_settings_json();
    assert_eq!(settings["hooks"]["PreToolUse"].as_array().unwrap().len(), 1);
}
```

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_no_claude_dir (CRITICAL - NotApplicable)
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_no_claude_dir_returns_not_applicable() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: no ~/.claude directory (user doesn't use Claude Code)
    assert!(!test_env.claude_dir().exists());
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: true };
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config).unwrap();
    
    // Assert: returns NotApplicable (NOT error, NOT creates dir)
    assert!(matches!(result, HookResult::NotApplicable));
    
    // Assert: ~/.claude was NOT created
    assert!(!test_env.claude_dir().exists());
}
```

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_claude_dir_exists_no_settings
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_claude_dir_exists_no_settings() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: ~/.claude exists but no settings.json
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    assert!(!test_env.settings_path().exists());
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: true };
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config).unwrap();
    
    // Assert: returns Installed
    assert!(matches!(result, HookResult::Installed));
    
    // Assert: settings.json created with hook
    let settings = test_env.read_settings_json();
    assert!(settings["hooks"]["PreToolUse"].as_array().unwrap()
        .iter().any(|h| h["command"] == "rch"));
}
```

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_preserves_existing
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_preserves_existing() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: settings.json with other hooks (e.g., DCG)
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    test_env.write_settings_json(json!({
        "hooks": {
            "PreToolUse": [
                {"command": "dcg", "description": "Destructive Command Guard"}
            ]
        },
        "other_setting": "preserved"
    }));
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: true };
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config).unwrap();
    
    // Assert: RCH hook added
    assert!(matches!(result, HookResult::Installed));
    
    // Assert: DCG hook still present
    let settings = test_env.read_settings_json();
    let hooks = settings["hooks"]["PreToolUse"].as_array().unwrap();
    assert!(hooks.iter().any(|h| h["command"] == "dcg"));
    assert!(hooks.iter().any(|h| h["command"] == "rch"));
    
    // Assert: other settings preserved
    assert_eq!(settings["other_setting"], "preserved");
}
```

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_malformed_json
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_malformed_json() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: settings.json with invalid JSON
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    std::fs::write(test_env.settings_path(), "{ invalid json }").unwrap();
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: true };
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config);
    
    // Assert: returns Err with clear message
    assert!(result.is_err());
    
    // Assert: file not modified (still malformed)
    let content = std::fs::read_to_string(test_env.settings_path()).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(content, "{ invalid json }");
}
```

#### Test: verify_and_install_hook_disabled_in_config
```rust
#[test]
fn test_verify_hook_disabled_in_config() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    
    // Setup: config with daemon_installs_hooks = false
    let config = HookConfig { daemon_installs_hooks: false };
    
    // Action: call verify_and_install_hook()
    let result = verify_and_install_claude_code_hook(&config).unwrap();
    
    // Assert: returns Skipped("disabled in config")
    assert!(matches!(result, HookResult::Skipped(msg) if msg.contains("disabled")));
}
```

### 3. Doctor Fix Tests (for bd-3pam, bd-2juk)

#### Test: doctor_fix_installs_missing_hook
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_doctor_fix_installs_hook() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    
    // Setup: no hook installed
    assert!(!test_env.settings_path().exists());
    
    // Action: run doctor with options.fix = true
    let options = DoctorOptions { fix: true, dry_run: false, ..Default::default() };
    let results = run_doctor(&options).await;
    
    // Assert: hook installed
    assert!(test_env.settings_path().exists());
    let settings = test_env.read_settings_json();
    assert!(settings["hooks"]["PreToolUse"].as_array().unwrap()
        .iter().any(|h| h["command"] == "rch"));
    
    // Assert: result shows fix_applied = true
    let hook_check = results.iter().find(|r| r.name == "claude_code_hook").unwrap();
    assert!(hook_check.fix_applied);
    assert_eq!(hook_check.status, CheckStatus::Pass);
}
```

#### Test: doctor_fix_dry_run_no_changes
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_doctor_fix_dry_run() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    test_env.create_claude_dir();
    
    // Setup: no hook installed
    assert!(!test_env.settings_path().exists());
    
    // Action: run doctor with options.fix = true, dry_run = true
    let options = DoctorOptions { fix: true, dry_run: true, ..Default::default() };
    let results = run_doctor(&options).await;
    
    // Assert: hook NOT installed
    assert!(!test_env.settings_path().exists());
    
    // Assert: result shows fix_message but fix_applied = false
    let hook_check = results.iter().find(|r| r.name == "claude_code_hook").unwrap();
    assert!(!hook_check.fix_applied);
    assert!(hook_check.fix_message.as_ref().unwrap().contains("Would"));
}
```

#### Test: doctor_fix_starts_daemon
```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_doctor_fix_starts_daemon() {
    let test_env = TestEnv::new();
    
    // Setup: daemon not running
    assert!(!test_env.socket_path().exists());
    
    // Action: run doctor with options.fix = true
    let options = DoctorOptions { fix: true, dry_run: false, ..Default::default() };
    let results = run_doctor(&options).await;
    
    // Assert: daemon check shows fix_applied = true
    let daemon_check = results.iter().find(|r| r.name == "daemon_running").unwrap();
    // Note: This may fail if rchd not available - handle in test
    if test_env.rchd_available() {
        assert!(daemon_check.fix_applied);
    }
    
    // Cleanup: stop daemon
    test_env.stop_daemon().await;
}
```

#### Test: config_defaults

```rust
#[test]
fn test_config_defaults() {

    assert!(config.hook_starts_daemon);
    assert!(config.daemon_installs_hooks);
    assert_eq!(config.daemon_start_timeout, 3);
    assert_eq!(config.auto_start_cooldown, 30);

}
```

#### Test: config_from_toml_partial
```rust
#[test]
fn test_config_partial() {
    let toml = r#"
        [self_healing]
        hook_starts_daemon = false
    "#;
    let config: RchConfig = toml::from_str(toml).unwrap();
    assert!(!config.self_healing.hook_starts_daemon);
    // Others should be defaults
    assert!(config.self_healing.daemon_installs_hooks);
    assert_eq!(config.self_healing.daemon_start_timeout, 3);
}
```

#### Test: config_env_override
```rust
#[test]
fn test_config_env_override() {
    let config = SelfHealingConfig::default();
    assert!(config.hook_starts_daemon);
    
    // Set env var
    std::env::set_var("RCH_HOOK_STARTS_DAEMON", "0");
    let config = config.with_env_overrides();
    assert!(!config.hook_starts_daemon);
    
    // Cleanup
    std::env::remove_var("RCH_HOOK_STARTS_DAEMON");
}
```

#### Test: config_env_master_disable
```rust
#[test]
fn test_config_env_master_disable() {
    let config = SelfHealingConfig::default();
    assert!(config.hook_starts_daemon);
    assert!(config.daemon_installs_hooks);
    
    // Set master disable
    std::env::set_var("RCH_NO_SELF_HEALING", "1");
    let config = config.with_env_overrides();
    assert!(!config.hook_starts_daemon);
    assert!(!config.daemon_installs_hooks);
    
    // Cleanup
    std::env::remove_var("RCH_NO_SELF_HEALING");
}
```

## Test Infrastructure

### TestEnv Helper
```rust
struct TestEnv {
    temp_dir: TempDir,
    original_home: String,
    original_xdg: Option<String>,
}

impl TestEnv {
    fn new() -> Self {
        let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
        let original_home = std::env::var("HOME").unwrap_or_default();
        let original_xdg = std::env::var("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR").ok();
        
        std::env::set_var("HOME", temp.path());
        std::env::set_var("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR", temp.path().join("run"));
        std::fs::create_dir_all(temp.path().join("run")).unwrap();
        
        Self { temp_dir: temp, original_home, original_xdg }
    }
    
    fn claude_dir(&self) -> PathBuf {
        self.temp_dir.path().join(".claude")
    }
    
    fn create_claude_dir(&self) {
        std::fs::create_dir_all(self.claude_dir()).unwrap();
    }
    
    fn settings_path(&self) -> PathBuf {
        self.claude_dir().join("settings.json")
    }
    
    fn socket_path(&self) -> &Path {
        // Return test socket path
    }
    
    fn lock_file(&self) -> PathBuf {
        self.temp_dir.path().join("run/rch-autostart.lock")
    }
    
    fn cooldown_file(&self) -> PathBuf {
        self.temp_dir.path().join("run/rch-autostart-cooldown")
    }
    
    fn write_settings_json(&self, value: serde_json::Value) {
        std::fs::write(
            self.settings_path(),
            serde_json::to_string_pretty(&value).unwrap()
        ).unwrap();
    }
    
    fn read_settings_json(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
        let content = std::fs::read_to_string(self.settings_path()).unwrap();
        serde_json::from_str(&content).unwrap()
    }
}

impl Drop for TestEnv {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        std::env::set_var("HOME", &self.original_home);
        if let Some(xdg) = &self.original_xdg {
            std::env::set_var("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR", xdg);
        } else {
            std::env::remove_var("XDG_RUNTIME_DIR");
        }
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All tests pass locally
- [ ] Tests are deterministic (no flaky tests)
- [ ] Tests clean up after themselves (temp dirs, env vars)
- [ ] Tests don't depend on external state
- [ ] Each test has clear setup/action/assert structure
- [ ] Critical test: NotApplicable when ~/.claude missing (not creates it!)
- [ ] Test coverage > 80% for self-healing code
- [ ] Config env override tests clean up env vars

## Code Locations
- `rch/src/hook.rs` or `rch/src/hook/autostart.rs` - #[cfg(test)] module
- `rch/src/doctor.rs` - Bottom of file, #[cfg(test)] module
- `rch/src/config.rs` - Bottom of file, #[cfg(test)] module
- `rch_common/src/hooks.rs` - Hook utility tests
- `tests/self_healing_unit.rs` - Cross-module integration tests","notes":"Added 12 comprehensive unit tests for self-healing system:

**Coverage:**
